From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Day for commemoration of all the faithful departed

"Holy Souls" redirects here. For other uses, see [Holy Souls (disambiguation)](/wiki/Holy_Souls_(disambiguation) "Holy Souls (disambiguation)"). "Faithful Departed" redirects here. For the Cranberries album, see [To the Faithful Departed](/wiki/To_the_Faithful_Departed "To the Faithful Departed").

For other uses, see [All Souls' Day (novel)](/wiki/All_Souls%27_Day_(novel) "All Souls' Day (novel)") and [All Souls Day (film)](/wiki/All_Souls_Day_(film) "All Souls Day (film)").

**All Souls' Day**[[a]](#cite_note-3) is a day of prayer and remembrance for the faithful departed,[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBregman201045-4) observed by [Christians](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") on 2 November.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrossLivingstone200542-5)[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBall200333-6) In [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity"), including [Roman Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") and certain parts of [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") and [Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism"), All Souls' Day is the third day of [Allhallowtide](/wiki/Allhallowtide "Allhallowtide"), after [All Saints' Day](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Day "All Saints' Day") (1 November) and [All Hallows' Eve](/wiki/Halloween "Halloween") (31 October).[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBannatyne199812-7) Before the standardization of Western Christian observance on 2 November by St. [Odilo of Cluny](/wiki/Odilo_of_Cluny "Odilo of Cluny") in the 10th century, many Roman Catholic congregations celebrated All Souls' Day on various dates during the [Easter season](/wiki/Easter_season "Easter season") as it is still observed in the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), the [Eastern Catholic](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic "Eastern Catholic") churches and the [Eastern Lutheran](/wiki/Eastern_Lutheran "Eastern Lutheran") churches. Churches of the [East Syriac Rite](/wiki/East_Syriac_Rite "East Syriac Rite") ([Assyrian Church of the East](/wiki/Assyrian_Church_of_the_East "Assyrian Church of the East"), [Ancient Church of the East](/wiki/Ancient_Church_of_the_East "Ancient Church of the East"), [Syro-Malabar Catholic Church](/wiki/Syro-Malabar_Catholic_Church "Syro-Malabar Catholic Church"), [Chaldean Catholic Church](/wiki/Chaldean_Catholic_Church "Chaldean Catholic Church"), [Syriac Catholic Church](/wiki/Syriac_Catholic_Church "Syriac Catholic Church")) commemorate all the faithful departed on the Friday before [Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent").[[7]](#cite_note-Economic2022-8) As with other days of the Allhallowtide season, popular practices for All Souls' Day include attending [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)") offered for the souls of the faithful departed, as well as Christian families visiting graveyards in order to pray and decorate their family graves with garlands, flowers, candles and incense.[[8]](#cite_note-UCA2014-9)[[9]](#cite_note-10)[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarkussen2013183-11) Given that many Christian cemeteries are interdenominational in nature, All Souls' Day observances often have an [ecumenical](/wiki/Ecumenical "Ecumenical") dimension, with believers from various Christian denominations praying together and cooperating to adorn graves.[[11]](#cite_note-TOI2012-12)[[12]](#cite_note-Vatican2015-13)

## In other languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=All_Souls%27_Day&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: In other languages")]

Known in [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") as *Commemoratio Omnium Fidelium Defunctorum*, All Souls' Day is known

* in other [Germanic languages](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages") as *Allerseelen* ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language")), *Allerzielen* ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language")), *Alla själars dag* ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language")), and *Alle Sjæles Dag* ([Danish](/wiki/Danish_language "Danish language"));
* in the [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages") as *Dia de Finados* or *Dia dos Fiéis Defuntos* ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language")), *Commémoration de tous les fidèles Défunts* ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language")), *Día de los Fieles Difuntos* ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language")), *Commemorazione di tutti i fedeli defunti* ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language")), and *Ziua morților* or *Luminația* ([Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language"));
* in the [Slavic languages](/wiki/Slavic_languages "Slavic languages") as *Wspomnienie Wszystkich Wiernych Zmarłych* or *Zaduszki* ([Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language")), *Vzpomínka na všechny věrné zesnulé*, *Památka zesnulých* or *Dušičky* ([Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language")), *Pamiatka zosnulých* or *Dušičky* ([Slovak](/wiki/Slovak_language "Slovak language")), *Spomen svih vjernih mrtvih* ([Croatian](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language")), and *День всех усопших верных* or *День поминовения всех усопших* (*Den' vsekh usopshikh vernykh*; *Den' pominoveniya vsekh usopshih*) ([Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"))
* in the [Baltic languages](/wiki/Baltic_languages "Baltic languages"): *Vėlinės* or *Visų Šventųjų Diena*
* in [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language"): *Halottak napja*
* and in [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"): *Dygwyl y Meirw*, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'Feast of the Dead'.

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=All_Souls%27_Day&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Background")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Non_bezoekt_kerkhof_met_Allerzielen_-_Nun_visiting_graveyard_at_All_Souls%27_Day_%286288419977%29.jpg/250px-Non_bezoekt_kerkhof_met_Allerzielen_-_Nun_visiting_graveyard_at_All_Souls%27_Day_%286288419977%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Non_bezoekt_kerkhof_met_Allerzielen_-_Nun_visiting_graveyard_at_All_Souls%27_Day_(6288419977).jpg)

Nun visiting a graveyard at All Souls' Day

In [Evangelical-Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") and [Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism"), the "faithful" refers to "all true believers on earth and in heaven, both living and dead" and during Allhallowtide, the "faithful departed" (the [Church Triumphant](/wiki/Churches_Militant,_Penitent,_and_Triumphant "Churches Militant, Penitent, and Triumphant")) are revered (cf. [communion of saints](/wiki/Communion_of_saints "Communion of saints")).[[13]](#cite_note-14)[[14]](#cite_note-15) In Evangelical Lutheranism, "the whole people of God in Christ Jesus" are seen as saints and All Souls' Day commemorates those believers who have died as the 'faithful departed'.[[15]](#cite_note-Joyocala-16)

In the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), "[the faithful](/wiki/Faithful_(baptized_Catholic) "Faithful (baptized Catholic)")" refers essentially to baptized Catholics; "all souls" commemorates the [church penitent](/wiki/Church_penitent "Church penitent") of souls in [purgatory](/wiki/Purgatory "Purgatory"), whereas "all saints" commemorates the [church triumphant](/wiki/Church_triumphant "Church triumphant") of [saints](/wiki/Saint "Saint") in [heaven](/wiki/Heaven_in_Christianity "Heaven in Christianity"). In the [liturgical books](/wiki/Liturgical_books_of_the_Roman_Rite "Liturgical books of the Roman Rite") of the [Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church") it is called the *Commemoration of All the Faithful Departed* ([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Commemoratio omnium fidelium defunctorum*). The Catholic Church teaches that the purification of the souls in purgatory can be assisted by the actions of the faithful on earth. Its teaching is based also on the practice of [prayer for the dead](/wiki/Prayer_for_the_dead "Prayer for the dead") mentioned as far back as 2 Maccabees 12:42–46.[[16]](#cite_note-CCC-17) The theological basis for the feast is the doctrine that the souls which, on departing from the body, are not perfectly cleansed from [venial sins](/wiki/Venial_sin "Venial sin"), or have not fully atoned for past transgressions, are debarred from the [Beatific vision](/wiki/Beatific_vision "Beatific vision"), and that the faithful on earth can help them by prayers, [alms](/wiki/Alms "Alms"), deeds, and especially by the sacrifice of the [Holy Mass](/wiki/Holy_Mass "Holy Mass").[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMershman1907-18)

The [United Protestant](/wiki/United_Protestant "United Protestant") tradition emphasizes "the Christian belief in bodily resurrection and eternal life" in observances of All Souls' Day.[[18]](#cite_note-Deshpande2023-19)

All Souls' Day is seen by many Christian leaders are one in which [ecumenism](/wiki/Ecumenism "Ecumenism") is celebrated, given that believers from various denominations collectively visit Christian cemeteries that are interdenominational in nature.[[12]](#cite_note-Vatican2015-13)[[11]](#cite_note-TOI2012-12) Christians from the Catholic, Lutheran, Reformed, Anglican, Methodist and Baptist denominations often come together to clean, repair and then decorate graveyards together.[[8]](#cite_note-UCA2014-9)[[11]](#cite_note-TOI2012-12) The [use of candles by Christians](/wiki/Ceremonial_use_of_lights#Christianity "Ceremonial use of lights") symbolized the [light of Christ](/wiki/Light_of_Christ "Light of Christ") and the use of lamps at the tombs of [Christian martyrs](/wiki/Christian_martyrs "Christian martyrs") dates back to the early Christian period.[[19]](#cite_note-20)[[20]](#cite_note-Beeton-21) Ecumenical prayer services are often held at Christian cemeteries on All Souls' Day.[[11]](#cite_note-TOI2012-12)

### Western Christianity

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Jakub_Schikaneder_-_All_Souls%27_Day.jpg/250px-Jakub_Schikaneder_-_All_Souls%27_Day.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jakub_Schikaneder_-_All_Souls%27_Day.jpg)

#### History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=All_Souls%27_Day&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: History")]

In [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity"), there is ample evidence of the custom of praying for the dead in the inscriptions of the [catacombs](/wiki/Catacomb "Catacomb"), with their constant prayers for the peace of the souls of the departed and in the early liturgies, which commonly contain commemorations of the dead. Tertullian, Cyprian and other early Western Fathers witness to the regular practice of praying for the dead among the early Christians.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrossLivingstone2005459-22)

In the sixth century, it was customary in Benedictine monasteries to hold a commemoration of the deceased members at Whitsuntide. In the time of [St. Isidore of Seville](/wiki/St._Isidore_of_Seville "St. Isidore of Seville") (d. 636) who lived in what is today Spain, the Monday after Pentecost was designated to remember the deceased. At the beginning of the ninth century, Abbot [Eigil of Fulda](/wiki/Eigil_of_Fulda "Eigil of Fulda") set 17 December as commemoration of all deceased in part of what is today Germany.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacDonald1967119-23)

According to [Widukind of Corvey](/wiki/Widukind_of_Corvey "Widukind of Corvey") (c. 975), there also existed a ceremony praying for the dead on 1 October in Saxony.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMershman1907-18) But it was the day after [All Saints' Day](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Day "All Saints' Day") that Saint [Odilo of Cluny](/wiki/Odilo_of_Cluny "Odilo of Cluny") chose when in the 11th century he instituted for all the monasteries dependent on the [Abbey of Cluny](/wiki/Abbey_of_Cluny "Abbey of Cluny") an annual commemoration of all the faithful departed, to be observed with alms, prayers, and sacrifices for the relief of the suffering souls in purgatory. Odilo decreed that those requesting a Mass be offered for the departed should make an offering for the poor, thus linking almsgiving with fasting and prayer for the dead.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEButler199012-24)

The 2 November date and customs spread from the Cluniac monasteries to other Benedictine monasteries and thence to the Western Church in general.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNamara2013-25) The [Diocese of Liège](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Roman Catholic Diocese of Liège") was the first diocese to adopt the practice under [Bishop Notger](/wiki/Notker_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Notker of Liège") (d. 1008).[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMershman1907-18) 2 November was adopted in Italy and Rome in the thirteenth century.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacDonald1967119-23)

In the 15th century the [Dominicans](/wiki/Dominican_Order "Dominican Order") instituted a custom of each priest offering three Masses on the Feast of All Souls. During World War I, given the great number of war dead and the many destroyed churches where Mass could no longer be said, [Pope Benedict XV](/wiki/Pope_Benedict_XV "Pope Benedict XV"), granted all priests the privilege of offering three Masses on All Souls' Day.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaunders2003-26)

#### Roman Catholicism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=All_Souls%27_Day&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Roman Catholicism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Skogskyrkog%C3%A5rden_at_All_Saints_Day_2010-2.jpg/250px-Skogskyrkog%C3%A5rden_at_All_Saints_Day_2010-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Skogskyrkog%C3%A5rden_at_All_Saints_Day_2010-2.jpg)

All Saints' Day at Skogskyrkogården in Stockholm. The graves are lighted with votive lights.

If 2 November falls on a Sunday, All Souls' Day is observed on that day. In the [Liturgy of the Hours](/wiki/Liturgy_of_the_Hours "Liturgy of the Hours") of All Souls' Day, the sequence *[Dies irae](/wiki/Dies_irae "Dies irae")* can be used *[ad libitum](/wiki/Ad_libitum "Ad libitum")*. Every priest is allowed to celebrate three holy Masses on All Souls' Day.

In [Divine Worship: The Missal](/wiki/Divine_Worship:_The_Missal "Divine Worship: The Missal"), used by members of the Anglican Ordinariates, the minor propers (Introit, Gradual, Tract, Sequence, Offertory, and Communion) are those used for Renaissance and Classical musical [requiem](/wiki/Requiem "Requiem") settings, including the [Dies Irae](/wiki/Dies_Irae "Dies Irae"). This permits the performance of traditional requiem settings in the context of the Divine Worship Form of the Roman Rite on All Souls' Day as well as at funerals, votive celebrations of all faithful departed, and anniversaries of deaths.[[26]](#cite_note-27)

In the ordinary form of the Roman Rite, as well as in the [Personal Ordinariates](/wiki/Personal_Ordinariates "Personal Ordinariates") established by [Benedict XVI](/wiki/Benedict_XVI "Benedict XVI") for former Anglicans, it remains on 2 November if this date falls on a Sunday;[[27]](#cite_note-28)[[28]](#cite_note-29) in the 1962–1969 form of the [Roman Rite](/wiki/Roman_Rite "Roman Rite"), use of which is still authorized, it is transferred to Monday, 3 November.[[29]](#cite_note-30)

According to the sacred tradition of the Catholic Church, from 1 to 8 November it is possible to gain a [plenary indulgence](/wiki/Plenary_indulgence "Plenary indulgence") for the benefit of the souls of the departed who are in [Purgatory](/wiki/Purgatory "Purgatory").[[30]](#cite_note-31) According to the *Enchiridion of Indulgences*, a plenary indulgence applicable only to the souls in purgatory (commonly called "the poor souls") is granted to the faithful who devoutly visit a cemetery (graveyard) and [pray for the dead](/wiki/Prayer_for_the_dead "Prayer for the dead").[[31]](#cite_note-Seton2024-32) The plenary indulgence can be gained between the second and ninth days of Allhallowtide (November 1–8); a partial indulgence is granted on other days of the year.[[31]](#cite_note-Seton2024-32) In order to gain the plenary indulgence, the Christian must have received [confession and absolution](/wiki/Confession_(religion)#Christianity "Confession (religion)") and the [eucharist](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist") twenty days before or after visiting the graveyard, in addition to praying for the intentions of the Pope.[[31]](#cite_note-Seton2024-32)

A plenary indulgence, applicable only to the poor souls, can be obtained by visiting a church, chapel or oratory on All Souls' Day and praying the [Lord's Prayer](/wiki/Lord%27s_Prayer "Lord's Prayer") there, along with the [Apostle's Creed](/wiki/Apostle%27s_Creed "Apostle's Creed"), [Athanasian Creed](/wiki/Athanasian_Creed "Athanasian Creed") or [Nicene Creed](/wiki/Nicene_Creed "Nicene Creed"). Alternatively, Christians can pray the Lauds or Vespers of the [Office of the Dead](/wiki/Office_of_the_Dead "Office of the Dead") and the [Eternal Rest](/wiki/Eternal_Rest "Eternal Rest") prayer for the dead.[[32]](#cite_note-EnchIndul-33)[[33]](#cite_note-catholic.org-34)

#### Evangelical-Lutheran Churches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=All_Souls%27_Day&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Evangelical-Lutheran Churches")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Alla_helgons_dag_vid_R%C3%B6ke_kyrka-1.jpg/250px-Alla_helgons_dag_vid_R%C3%B6ke_kyrka-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alla_helgons_dag_vid_R%C3%B6ke_kyrka-1.jpg)

A [graveyard](/wiki/Churchyard "Churchyard") outside a [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") church in the Swedish city of [Röke](/wiki/R%C3%B6ke "Röke") during Allhallowtide

All Souls Day is observed in the [Evangelical-Lutheran Churches](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism").[[34]](#cite_note-FastThomas2004-35) During [Luther's](/wiki/Martin_Luther_(religious_leader) "Martin Luther (religious leader)") lifetime, All Souls' Day was widely observed in [Saxony](/wiki/Saxony "Saxony");[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911-36) ecclesiastically in the [Lutheran Church](/wiki/Lutheran_Church "Lutheran Church"), the day was merged with and is often seen as an extension of All Saints' Day, with many Lutherans still visiting and decorating graves on all the days of Allhallowtide, including All Souls' Day.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarkussen2013183-11) In the Lutheran Churches, "the whole people of God in Christ Jesus" are seen as saints and All Souls' Day commemorates those believers who have died as the 'faithful departed'.[[15]](#cite_note-Joyocala-16) Just as it is the custom of [French people](/wiki/French_people "French people"), of all ranks and creeds, to decorate the graves of their dead on the *jour des morts*, [Germans](/wiki/German_people "German people") come to the graveyards on All Souls' Day with offerings of flowers and special grave lights.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911-36) In Sweden, an Evangelical-Lutheran country, "people gather in churches lit with candles and decorated with wreaths to remember their loved ones" and "also visit cemeteries and leave special lanterns on people's graves that burn well into the night."[[34]](#cite_note-FastThomas2004-35) An Evangelical Lutheran prayer said on All Souls' Day is as follows:

> I would remember before Thee also my parents, pastors, teachers, children, kindred and benefactors, who have gone before me in the blessed faith and are now at home with Thee. If, through Jesus Christ, my prayer finds favor in Thy sight, do Thou, in my stead, repay unto them my thanks and love, in whatever manner it be possible. Unto all whom I have ever pained, deceived, or caused to sin, or whom I have robbed of honor, health, or possessions, whom I can no longer ask for pardon, nor restore unto them, because they already are gone into joy and pardon of every sin—gone home to Thee—to all these, O Lord, grant good for all my evil, both now and in the day of the resurrection of the just; even as Thou knowest how, and in how far all this which I ask can be granted. As for myself, let me spend my remaining days in prayer, in adoration of the most holy name of Jesus, and in praise and thanksgiving for the hearing of my prayers and those of all Christian people which have ever been offered up unto Thee through Jesus Christ. Amen.[[36]](#cite_note-Weedon2019-37)

#### Anglican Communion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=All_Souls%27_Day&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Anglican Communion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Leichhardt_All_Souls_Anglican_Church.JPG/250px-Leichhardt_All_Souls_Anglican_Church.JPG)](/wiki/File:Leichhardt_All_Souls_Anglican_Church.JPG)

[All Souls Anglican Church](/wiki/All_Souls_Anglican_Church,_Leichhardt "All Souls Anglican Church, Leichhardt") in the [Diocese of Sydney](/wiki/Anglican_Diocese_of_Sydney "Anglican Diocese of Sydney"), a parish dedicated to All Souls

In the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") it is called The Commemoration of the Faithful Departed and is an optional celebration; Anglicans view All Souls' Day as an extension of the observance of All Saints' Day and it serves to "remember those who have died", in connection with the theological doctrines of the [resurrection of the body](/wiki/Resurrection_of_the_dead "Resurrection of the dead") and the [Communion of Saints](/wiki/Communion_of_Saints "Communion of Saints").[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaysHancock2012128-38)[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArmentroutSlocum19997-39)

In the [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion"), All Souls' Day is known liturgically as the Commemoration of All Faithful Departed, and is an optional observance seen as "an extension of All Saints' Day", the latter of which marks the second day of [Allhallowtide](/wiki/Allhallowtide "Allhallowtide").[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArmentroutSlocum19997-39)[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDickison2014-40) Historically and at present, several Anglican churches are dedicated to All Souls. During the [English Reformation](/wiki/English_Reformation "English Reformation"), the observance of All Souls' Day lapsed, although a new Anglican theological understanding of the day has "led to a widespread acceptance of this commemoration among Anglicans".[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMichno1998160-41) Patricia Bays, with regard to the Anglican view of All Souls' Day, wrote that:[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaysHancock2012128-38)

> All Souls Day … is a time when we particularly remember those who have died. The prayers appointed for that day remind us that we are joined with the [Communion of Saints](/wiki/Communion_of_Saints "Communion of Saints"), that great group of Christians who have finished their earthly life and with who we share the hope of [resurrection from the dead](/wiki/Resurrection_of_the_dead "Resurrection of the dead").

— [Bays & Hancock 2012](#CITEREFBaysHancock2012), p. 128

As such, Anglican parishes "now commemorate all the faithful departed in the context of the All Saints' Day celebration", in keeping with this fresh perspective.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArmentroutSlocum19997-39) Contributing to the revival was the need "to help Anglicans mourn the deaths of millions of soldiers in [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I")".[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEnglish20044-42) Members of the [Guild of All Souls](/wiki/Guild_of_All_Souls "Guild of All Souls"), an [Anglican devotional society](/wiki/Anglican_devotional_society "Anglican devotional society") founded in 1873, "are encouraged to pray for the dying and the dead, to participate in a requiem of All Souls' Day and say a Litany of the Faithful Departed at least once a month".[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArmentroutSlocum1999232-43)

At the [Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") the celebration of All Souls' Day was fused with [All Saints' Day](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Day "All Saints' Day") in the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England")[[43]](#cite_note-Episcopal-44) or, in the judgement of some, it was "deservedly abrogated".[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBCP1850-45) It was reinstated in certain parishes in connection with the [Oxford Movement](/wiki/Oxford_Movement "Oxford Movement") of the 19th century[[43]](#cite_note-Episcopal-44) and is acknowledged in United States Anglicanism in the *Holy Women, Holy Men* calendar[[43]](#cite_note-Episcopal-44) and in the Church of England with the 1980 *[Alternative Service Book](/wiki/Alternative_Service_Book "Alternative Service Book")*. It features in *[Common Worship](/wiki/Common_Worship "Common Worship")* as a [Lesser Festival](/wiki/Lesser_Festival "Lesser Festival") called "Commemoration of the Faithful Departed (All Souls' Day)".[[45]](#cite_note-churchofengland-46)

#### Reformed churches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=All_Souls%27_Day&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Reformed churches")]

Certain [Reformed](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism") ([Continental Reformed](/wiki/Continental_Reformed "Continental Reformed"), [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian"), and [Congregationalist](/wiki/Congregationalist "Congregationalist")) churches observe All Souls' Day.[[11]](#cite_note-TOI2012-12) In All Souls' Day observances by the Reformed Churches, the theological doctrine of "the Christian belief in bodily resurrection and eternal life" is emphasized, along with a remembrance of the faithful departed.[[18]](#cite_note-Deshpande2023-19) Additionally, dead are remembered on the feast of [Totensonntag](/wiki/Totensonntag "Totensonntag") (Totenfest), the last Sunday before [Advent](/wiki/Advent "Advent"). It was introduced in 1816, in [Prussia](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia"), and in addition to the Reformed, it is observed by Lutherans in addition to Allhallowtide, particularly in areas with a large Germanic presence.[[46]](#cite_note-47)

#### Methodist churches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=All_Souls%27_Day&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Methodist churches")]

In the [Methodist Church](/wiki/Methodist_Church "Methodist Church"), [saints](/wiki/Saints_in_Methodism "Saints in Methodism") refer to all [Christians](/wiki/Christians "Christians") and therefore, on [All Saints' Day](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Day "All Saints' Day"), the [Church Universal](/wiki/Church_Universal "Church Universal"), as well as the deceased members of a [local congregation](/wiki/Local_congregation "Local congregation") are honoured and remembered.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHileman2003-48)[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPeck2011-49) In Methodist congregations that celebrate the liturgy on All Souls' Day, the observance, as with Anglicanism and Lutheranism, is viewed as an extension of All Saints' Day and as such, Methodists "remember our loved ones who had died" in their observance of this feast.[[49]](#cite_note-sheringhammethodist.org.uk-50)

### Eastern Catholic, Eastern Lutheran and Eastern Orthodox

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=All_Souls%27_Day&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Eastern Catholic, Eastern Lutheran and Eastern Orthodox")]

Main article: [Saturday of Souls](/wiki/Saturday_of_Souls "Saturday of Souls")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Soul_Saturday_Kollyva_offerings.jpg/250px-Soul_Saturday_Kollyva_offerings.jpg)](/wiki/File:Soul_Saturday_Kollyva_offerings.jpg)

Kollyva offerings of boiled wheat blessed liturgically on Soul Saturday (*Psychosabbaton*)

[Saturday of Souls](/wiki/Saturday_of_Souls "Saturday of Souls") (or Soul Saturday) is a day set aside for the commemoration of the dead within the [liturgical year](/wiki/Liturgical_year "Liturgical year") of the [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox"), Eastern Lutheran and Byzantine Catholic Churches.[[7]](#cite_note-Economic2022-8) Saturday is a traditional day of prayer for the dead, because Christ lay dead in the [Tomb](/wiki/Holy_Sepulchre "Holy Sepulchre") on Saturday.[[50]](#cite_note-GOArch-51)

These days are devoted to prayer for departed relatives and others among the faithful who would not be commemorated specifically as saints. The [Divine Services](/wiki/Divine_Services "Divine Services") on these days have special hymns added to them to commemorate the departed. There is often a [Panikhida](/wiki/Panikhida "Panikhida") (Memorial Service) either after the [Divine Liturgy](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy "Divine Liturgy") on Saturday morning or after [Vespers](/wiki/Vespers "Vespers") on Friday evening, for which [Koliva](/wiki/Koliva "Koliva") (a dish made of boiled wheatberries or rice and honey) is prepared and placed on the Panikhida table. After the Service, the priest blesses the Koliva. It is then eaten as a memorial by all present.[[51]](#cite_note-orthodoxpath-52)

#### Radonitsa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=All_Souls%27_Day&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Radonitsa")]

Main article: [Radonitsa](/wiki/Radonitsa "Radonitsa")

Another Memorial Day in the East, Radonitsa, does not fall on a Saturday, but on either Monday or Tuesday of the second week after Pascha (Easter).[[52]](#cite_note-Averky-53)[[53]](#cite_note-Bulgakov_Handbook-54) Radonitsa does not have special hymns for the dead at the Divine Services. Instead a Panikhida will follow the Divine Liturgy, and then all will bring paschal foods to the cemeteries to greet the departed with the joy of the Resurrection.[[52]](#cite_note-Averky-53)

### East Syriac tradition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=All_Souls%27_Day&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: East Syriac tradition")]

East Syriac churches including the [Syro Malabar Church](/wiki/Syro_Malabar_Church "Syro Malabar Church") and [Chaldean Catholic Church](/wiki/Chaldean_Catholic_Church "Chaldean Catholic Church") commemorates the feast of departed faithful on the last Friday of [Epiphany season](/wiki/Epiphany_season "Epiphany season") (which means Friday just before start of [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent")).[[54]](#cite_note-nasranifoundation.org-55) The season of Epiphany remembers the revelation of Christ to the world. Each Friday of Epiphany season, the church remembers important evangelistic figures.[[55]](#cite_note-syromalabarchurch.in-56)

In the [Syro Malabar Church](/wiki/Syro_Malabar_Church "Syro Malabar Church"), the Friday before the parish festival is also celebrated as feast of departed faithful when the parish remembers the activities of forebears who worked for the parish and faithful. They also request the intercession of all departed souls for the faithful celebration of parish festival. In East Syriac liturgy, the church remembers departed souls including saints on every Friday throughout the year since the Christ was crucified and died on Friday.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Popular customs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=All_Souls%27_Day&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Popular customs")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Philippine_graveyard%2C_All_Souls%27_Day.jpg/250px-Philippine_graveyard%2C_All_Souls%27_Day.jpg)](/wiki/File:Philippine_graveyard,_All_Souls%27_Day.jpg)

A cemetery on All Souls' Day in the Philippines, with flowers left after the festivities of the previous night (All Souls' Day eve on All Saints' Day).

On All Souls' Day, Christians of various denominational backgrounds, including Catholics, Lutherans, Anglicans and Methodists, among others, often help one another clean the graves of cemeteries, along with adorning them with flowers.[[8]](#cite_note-UCA2014-9) The General Secretary of the [Church of North India](/wiki/Church_of_North_India "Church of North India") described the [ecumenism](/wiki/Ecumenism "Ecumenism") present in All Souls' Day, stating that "This coming together shows Christian unity".[[8]](#cite_note-UCA2014-9) With respect to the economy, vendors "sell flowers, candles and incense sticks" to those visiting the graveyards, who are Christians of the Catholic and Protestant traditions.[[8]](#cite_note-UCA2014-9) Prayer services with representatives from different Christian denomination for All Souls' Day are held at graveyards for those visiting them.[[11]](#cite_note-TOI2012-12)[[12]](#cite_note-Vatican2015-13)

All Souls' Day emphasizes "the Christian belief in bodily resurrection and eternal life".[[18]](#cite_note-Deshpande2023-19) Some All Souls' Day traditions are associated with the doctrine of the poor souls of [purgatory](/wiki/Purgatory "Purgatory") (in Roman Catholicism) or the [intermediate state](/wiki/Intermediate_state_(Christianity) "Intermediate state (Christianity)") (in Protestantism and Orthodoxy). [Church bell](/wiki/Church_bell "Church bell") tolling is done in honour of the dead. Lighting candles serves variously to kindle a light for the poor souls, honour the dead, as well as to ward off demons.[[56]](#cite_note-57)[[57]](#cite_note-CarterPetro1998-58) [Soul cakes](/wiki/Soul_cakes "Soul cakes") are given to children going souling—going from door to door to [pray for the dead](/wiki/Prayer_for_the_dead "Prayer for the dead") (cf. *[trick-or-treating](/wiki/Trick-or-treating "Trick-or-treating")*, *[Pão-por-Deus](/wiki/P%C3%A3o-por-Deus "Pão-por-Deus")*).[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchousboe201210–13-59)[[59]](#cite_note-Mosteller-60)

### Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=All_Souls%27_Day&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Europe")]

All Souls' Day is celebrated in many European countries with vigils, candles, the decoration of graves, and special prayers as well as many regional customs.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911-36) Examples of regional customs include leaving cakes for departed loved ones on the table and keeping the room warm for their comfort in [Tirol](/wiki/Tyrol_(state) "Tyrol (state)") and the custom in [Brittany](/wiki/Brittany "Brittany"), where people flock to the cemeteries at nightfall to kneel, bareheaded, at the graves of their loved ones and anoint the hollow of the [tombstone](/wiki/Tomb_stone "Tomb stone") with [holy water](/wiki/Holy_water "Holy water") or to pour libations of milk on it. At bedtime, supper is left on the table for the souls.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911-36) All Souls' Day is known in [Maltese](/wiki/Maltese_language "Maltese language") as *Jum il-Mejtin*, and is accompanied a traditional supper including roasted pig, based on a custom of letting a pig loose on the streets with a bell around its neck, to be fed by the entire neighborhood and cooked on that day to feed the poor.[[60]](#cite_note-ToM-61) In [Linz](/wiki/Linz "Linz"), funereal musical pieces known as [aequales](/wiki/Aequale "Aequale") were played from tower tops on All Souls' Day and the evening before.[[61]](#cite_note-triton-62) In the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic") and [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia") All Souls' Day is called Dušičky, or "little souls". Traditionally, candles are left on graves on Dušičky.[[62]](#cite_note-63) In Sicily and other regions of southern Italy, All Souls' Day is celebrated as the *Festa dei Morti* or *U juornu rii morti*, the "Commemoration of the Dead" or the "Day of the Dead", which according to Joshua Nicolosi of the *Sicilian Post* could be seen "halfway between Christian and pagan traditions".[[63]](#cite_note-64)[[64]](#cite_note-:0-65) Families visit and clean grave sites, home altars are decorated with family photos and votive candles, and children are gifted a special basket or *cannistru* of chocolates, pomegranate, and other gifts from their ancestors.[[65]](#cite_note-66)[[64]](#cite_note-:0-65) Because of the gifting of sugary sweets and the emphasis on sugar puppet decorations, the Commemoration Day has spurred local Sicilian events such as the *Notte di Zucchero* ("Night of Sugar") in which communities celebrate the dead.[[66]](#cite_note-67) *[Piada dei morti](/wiki/Piada_dei_morti "Piada dei morti")* (lit. 'piada of the dead'), a sweet [focaccia](/wiki/Focaccia "Focaccia") topped with [raisins](/wiki/Raisin "Raisin"), [almonds](/wiki/Almond "Almond"), [walnuts](/wiki/Walnut "Walnut"), and [pine nuts](/wiki/Pine_nut "Pine nut"),[[67]](#cite_note-:3-68)[[68]](#cite_note-:4-69) is traditionally eaten in November for All Souls' Day in the environs of [Rimini](/wiki/Rimini "Rimini"), in [Emilia-Romagna](/wiki/Emilia-Romagna "Emilia-Romagna").[[67]](#cite_note-:3-68)

### Indian subcontinent

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=All_Souls%27_Day&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Indian subcontinent")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Hallowmas_India_1.jpg/250px-Hallowmas_India_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hallowmas_India_1.jpg)

A Christian woman in India decorates a grave with flower petals.

In the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") (India, Pakistan and Bangladesh), Christians hold prayer services in which they pray for the faithful departed, especially remembering their loved ones.[[18]](#cite_note-Deshpande2023-19)[[12]](#cite_note-Vatican2015-13) Christians of various denominations visit cemeteries and adorn graves with flower petals, garlands, candles and incense sticks.[[18]](#cite_note-Deshpande2023-19)[[12]](#cite_note-Vatican2015-13)

Main article: [All Saints' Day § Philippines](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Day#Philippines "All Saints' Day")

In the Philippines, Hallow mas is variously called "*Undás*", "*Todos los Santos*" (Spanish, "All Saints"), and sometimes "*Araw ng mga Patay / Yumao*" ([Tagalog](/wiki/Tagalog_language "Tagalog language"), "Day of the dead / those who have passed away"), which incorporates All Saints' Day and All Souls' Day.[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:All_Souls%27_Day#Dubious "Talk:All Souls' Day")*] Filipinos traditionally observe this day by visiting the family dead to clean and repair their tombs. Offerings of prayers, flowers, candles,[[69]](#cite_note-guardian-world-saints-70) and food. [Chinese Filipinos](/wiki/Chinese_Filipinos "Chinese Filipinos") additionally burn [incense](/wiki/Joss_stick "Joss stick") and *[kim](/wiki/Joss_paper "Joss paper")*. Many also spend the day and ensuing night holding reunions at the cemetery with feasts and merriment.
